s2.q: Fetal macrosomia is defined as weight greater than:
s2.explain: Macrosomia is defined as fetal weight > 4,000 grams.

s2.q: Which of the following best defines this condition?
options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s2.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s2.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s2.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

s3.q: Which is a risk factor for fetal macrosomia?
s3.explain: Risk factors: history of macrosomia, obesity, excessive weight gain, multiparity, male infants, and maternal age > 35.

s3.q: Male infants are at higher risk for macrosomia because:
s3.explain: Male infants typically weigh slightly more than female infants, increasing macrosomia risk.

s3.q: The risk of macrosomia increases with:
s3.explain: The risk of macrosomia increases with each subsequent pregnancy.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s4.q: Which maternal condition can cause fetal macrosomia?
s4.explain: Maternal diabetes can cause fetal macrosomia. Other causes: genetic factors, multiparity, and postterm pregnancy.

s4.q: All are causes of macrosomia EXCEPT:
s4.explain: Placental insufficiency is associated with IUGR, not macrosomia.

s4.q: Which is a cause of macrosomia?
s4.explain: Postterm pregnancy causes continued fetal growth beyond term, resulting in a large-for-gestational-age fetus.

s4.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s4.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: Which is a clinical feature of macrosomia?
s5.explain: Clinical features: large fundal height and excessive amniotic fluid (polyhydramnios).

s5.q: Which finding on examination may suggest macrosomia?
s5.explain: Polyhydramnios (excessive AF) is a clinical feature of macrosomia along with large fundal height.

s5.q: The most common presenting symptom is:
s5.explain: Clinical presentation varies — pain and bleeding are common but not universal symptoms.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s5.q: Which sign on examination is characteristic?
s5.explain: Examination findings depend heavily on the specific obstetric condition.

s6.q: Which is a maternal complication of macrosomia?
s6.explain: PPH is a maternal complication. Fetal injuries, meconium aspiration, and shoulder dystocia are fetal/neonatal complications.

s6.q: Shoulder dystocia in macrosomia can cause:
options: Fetal injuries including fractures and brachial plexus injury
s6.explain: Shoulder dystocia can cause fetal injuries including fractures and brachial plexus injuries.

s6.q: All are complications of macrosomia EXCEPT:
s6.explain: IUGR/fetal growth restriction is the opposite condition. Macrosomia complications include shoulder dystocia, fetal injuries, PPH, and uterine rupture.

s6.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s6.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s6.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s6.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s7.q: Which delivery mode is recommended for macrosomia?
s7.explain: Cesarean section is safe for both mother and fetus in cases of macrosomia.

s7.q: Which is part of antenatal management of macrosomia?
options: Proper antenatal care to prevent and diagnose before labor
s7.explain: Proper antenatal care aims to prevent macrosomia and diagnose it before labor.

s7.q: Postnatal management of a macrosomic neonate includes:
options: Proper examination and management of any problem
s7.explain: Postnatal management involves proper examination of the neonate and management of any identified problems.

s7.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s7.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.
Conservative management is reserved for mild, uncomplicated cases.
